responseSet.title: Yesterday, Today and Tomorrow
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Yesterday, Today and Tomorrow

Tennyson Lee

Tennyson Lee

Tennyson Lee  

by Hanna Segal, edited by Nicola Abel-Hirsch, London, Routledge and the Institute of Psychoanalysis, 2007, 280 pp., £21.99 (paperback).

What relevance has psychoanalysis today in the post-September 11 world, with the rise of fundamentalism and the accompanying certainty of a single ‘Truth’? Dr Segal clearly sees a role for psychoanalysis in all of this. She suggests ‘From the humane (including psychoanalytic) point of view we are led as citizens to struggle with the unending task of exposing lies and striving for the preservation of sane human values’ (p. 45).

In Dr Segal's obituary of Melanie Klein (written together with Rosenfeld and Bion, 1961) she wrote ‘All scientific work has as its aim to see life “as it is”. The peculiarity of psychoanalysis lies in our belief that such an aim and its steady pursuit are restorative’. This emphasis on truth and, more importantly, how it is acquired (or not), links together many of the papers in this book. Yesterday, today and tomorrow presents a generous slice of the work of Dr Segal – both chronologically and in breadth. As someone who has not read a lot of her work, I approached it with a bit of trepidation – would I be able to appreciate it without having read her earlier work? In brief I did – Dr Segal's writing is lucid and generous – those new to her thinking are welcomed in and get a tour de force of ideas – bracing yes, but also embracing.

The book is divided into two parts. Part 1 contains the main papers, dating from 2000–2006. Part 2 covers Dr Segal's contributions to symposia, conferences and occasional writings dating from 1972–2000, and includes sections on models of the mind, psychoanalytic technique, on Klein and Bion.

In the first paper of the book ‘Interpretation of dreams, 100 years on’ (2000) Dr Segal distinguishes between dreaming in psychotic and non-psychotic functioning. She believes that in psychotic functioning, there is a failure of symbolization and the dreams fulfil the function of evacuation of psychic conflicts rather than working through them. The relationship between the psychotic and non-psychotic world is again explored in the second paper ‘Disillusionment: The story of Adam and Eve and that of Lucifer’ (2000), based on John Milton's Paradise lost. In this piece, Segal views the response of Adam and Eve to their fall from paradise as non-psychotic in their acceptance of their expulsion into ‘ordinariness’. She believes, however, that Lucifer's response to his fall is a psychotic one – filled with pride and envy he creates an omnipotent, paranoid system.

ANNUAL ESSAY PRIZE

THE ASSOCIATION FOR PSYCHOANALYTIC PSYCHOTHERAPY IN THE NHS

The Educational Trust of the APP offers an Annual Essay Prize of £1000 for the best essay submitted on the theme of the clinical application of psychoanalytic thinking in the public sector.

We expect to publish the winning paper and runners-up in the APP journal Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy.

Essays will be judged by members of the Educational Trust and Members of the Editorial Board of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy.

Essays will be considered provided they have not been previously published or submitted for publication.

Applicants should be members of the A.P.P.

Entries to: Dr. Maureen Marks

  PO71, Institute of Psychiatry De Crespigny Park

London SE5 8AF

  e-mail: m.marks@iop.kcl.ac.uk

Closing date for the submission of essays: 31st December 2008

responseSet.title: A Kleinian view of psychoanalytic couples therapy: Part 2
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Kleinian view of psychoanalytic couples therapy: Part 2

Robert Waska

Robert Waska

Robert Waska  

With extensive case material, Part 2 continues to explore the psychoanalytic treatment of couples. Since most couples enter treatment operating in a more primitive paranoid-schizoid stance, the author contends that it is best to initially focus on each partner's pathological projections and have the other partner witness their struggle to overcome personal issues that contaminate the couple's unified psychology. With continuous working through, the couple can gradually find a more depressive, integrative footing within themselves and as a couple. Again, the value of ‘witnessing’ and working through individual defensive reactions against thinking, pathological projective identifications and the breakdown of the container-contained function are all examined in the clinical presentations. All these clinical elements are part of the establishment of analytic contact first with each party as individuals and later as a unified couple.

responseSet.title: The Couch and the Silver Screen. Psychoanalytic Reflections on European Cinema, edited by Andrea Sabbadini, London & New York, Brunner-Routledge, 2003, 280 pp., £21.99
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Couch and the Silver Screen. Psychoanalytic Reflections on European Cinema, edited by Andrea Sabbadini, London & New York, Brunner-Routledge, 2003, 280 pp., £21.99

Maria Eyres

Maria Eyres

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3
 Email: meyres@tavi‐port.nhs.uk

Maria Eyres  

There is a certain nostalgic magic about the title of this book, echoed in its slightly out of focus cover, with an old fashioned movie camera and the reels of film, the magic that takes the reader straight into the world of celluloid. The world is illuminated by the light beam from the projector with the particles of dust dancing through it, with a lingering smell of popcorn, Hollywood glamour and high drama.

The content of the book grew out of the First European Psychoanalytic Film Festival that took place in November 2001 under the chairmanship of the book's editor, Andrea Sabbadini. The volume belongs to the New Library of Psychoanalysis series.

I was certainly interested but quietly sceptical as well; can the book really reflect the atmosphere of such a complex and rich event; how possible it is to capture the spirit of the moving pictures in the stillness of the written word?

An Introduction by Sabbadini explores the relationship between the cinema and psychoanalysis, the way they influence and inform each other and how psychoanalysis can be used to enquire into the art of the cinema.

The book is divided into four parts; ‘Set and stage’, ‘Working through trauma’, ‘Horror perspectives’ and ‘Documenting internal world’. Each part is then divided into chapters and the contributors include actors, directors, producers, analysts, psychotherapists and film scholars.

‘Set and stage’ follows the Introduction with transcripts from two meetings of thespians and analysts, starting with Bernardo Bertolucci, Fiona Show and Chris Mowson discussing the inner and outer world of the film-maker's temporary inner structure. Bertolucci explains how in 1976, during the making of the film entitled ‘1900’ he came to accept that the movie is not a solitary opus but an expression and a collective creation of whole of the crew colony involved in its making. He likened the group dynamics to a kind of Uutopian, independent republic, where the crew leads almost a second life and becomes so submerged in this fantasy that finishing the film is quite difficult; the denial of external reality and separation anxiety clearly present. He also described how at times the stories from the set seem to be in competition with story in the film. Fiona Show compares making a film to being a part of a big, extended family.

responseSet.title: The patient in hospital*
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The patient in hospital*

Michael Conran

Michael Conran

Michael Conran  

The application of psychoanalytic knowledge to the care of the patient in hospital is considered in relation to two patients, one an acute surgical admission, the other psychotic. The psychological parallel is examined and defined before proceeding to where they diverge. The psychotic patient's use of splitting and projective identification, in order to avoid psychic pain felt to be incompatible with survival, is described along with the hospital staff's unconscious and empirical collusion with it. The patient's need to be ill, which is his right to be ill in hospital, is considered in relation to the psychotic's denial of his need. The inseparability of the care of the patient's body and mind, in these circumstances, is also illustrated, and argued to be equally germane to psychoanalytic thinking in this setting. It is submitted that the application of psychoanalytic knowledge catalyses and sustains relationship with these patients. The use of interpretation is briefly noted.

The paper is an attempt to stimulate thought and other publications in this field, one of lasting relevance to psychoanalytic psychotherapists working in the National Health Service.

responseSet.title: 111 Common therapeutic blunders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

111 Common therapeutic blunders

edited by Gerald Schoenewolf, Northvale, NJ, Jason Aronson, 2005, 246 pp.

I enjoyed reading this book. In the Preface of this, the second edition, Gerald Schoenewolf relates the history behind the rather frivolous-sounding title as being a serious and ambitious attempt to explore counter-transference and counter-resistance errors in therapy. The first edition of this book apparently became an instant success and the new edition with new chapters on different aspects of counter-transferences was felt to be necessary to reflect ‘new situations created by the increased diversity of patients and the intrusion of insurance companies into the therapy equation’.

He goes on to define counter-transference and counter-resistance, emphasizing his use of the terms in an attempt to make them inclusive of all of the therapist's feelings, thoughts or impulses about a patient and counter-resistance for all instances in which the therapist acts out on such feelings whether or not they are induced by the patient. He also discusses objective and subjective aspects of counter-transference and counter-resistance.

One of the things that is immediately apparent in reading this book, is the ease and simplicity of the material. The vignettes are short, to the point and interspersed with aspects of theory which relate to the clinical material being discussed. They are also frequently amusing and gently entertaining. After going through the book one has a sense of a ‘manual of methods’, such as those so popular with some of the other therapeutic disciplines within psychotherapy. This feels like a strength, which would make it of use to both supervisees and supervisors. This would be a useful and accessible inclusion on a reading list for trainee therapists to remind them of the continuing nature of enquiry which is so essential in clinical work in psychoanalysis, but also very much in the ongoing investigation of the therapist's internal world, without which therapy becomes simply an intellectual exercise. ‘The therapist's manifesto’ at the end of the book movingly captures the challenges facing anyone who wishes to be a therapist, either in training or even later in a senior position. Equal scrutiny to all the layers of the work, the trainee and the senior experienced therapist, along with the author's own examples of blunders, makes this a refreshing book.

responseSet.title: Psychosomatics: The uses of psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychosomatics: The uses of psychotherapy

Psychosomatics: The uses of psychotherapy

by Peter Shoenberg, (Palgrave Macmillan, 2007, £19.95, 272pp plus xvi)

‘Psychosomatic medicine’ is a rather unfashionable term. Historically, it has been associated with hypnotism and with the idea that there is a circumscribed set of ‘psychosomatic’ disorders. In the hands of Dunbar, Alexander and French, these were syndromes that had physical signs and symptoms, posing a serious threat to health, which could be alleviated by psychoanalytic treatment, provided this was of sufficient depth. The steady displacement of ‘psychosomatic medicine’ by ‘health psychology’ has brought two kinds of problems. One is a tendency to restrict its clinical scope in a different way, as another professional coterie takes the place of those medical psychosomatists. The other has been an exclusive reliance on psychological and social research with clinical populations in understanding the development of illness and individuals' reactions to it, to the exclusion of insights based on developmental idiosyncracies and findings from psychotherapeutic exploration.

In writing a book which manages to temper clinical research with psychodynamic understanding, Peter Shoenberg redresses this balance. After a historical introduction that would have delighted the psychosomatists of fifty years ago, Psychosomatics provides brief summaries of diagnosable conditions sensitive to the psychodynamics that are frequently encountered. Unlike the earlier psychosomatists, he is able to set out the prospects for benefit from a broad range of psychotherapeutic treatments. According to Schoenberg, the main psychosomatic targets have become: stress; somatoform disorders; obesity and eating disorders and headache, as well as specific gastrointestinal, skin, cardiovascular, respiratory and musculoskeletal and gynaecological syndromes. Given the considerable psychiatric and psychotherapeutic literature on the earlier topics, the chapters on the last six kinds of systemic disorder are possibly the most original and interesting. Before ending the book, Schoenberg also considers some issues generic to psychological understanding of the cancers, although some may feel this brief section only partially reflects how rich the work and literature on therapeutic approaches within psycho-oncology have become.

responseSet.title: Tuning into the psychotic wavelength: Psychoanalytic supervision for mental health professionals
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tuning into the psychotic wavelength: Psychoanalytic supervision for mental health professionals

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans  

psychotic wavelength, psychoanalytic, concrete communication, transference, countertransference

In this paper, material is presented from supervision groups run for mental health professionals in different mental health settings. I will argue that a psychoanalytic framework, which puts the transference and countertransference at the centre of clinical practice, offers an important model for thinking about psychosis and psychotic means of communication. When staff are able to ‘tune in’ to their patients' communications it can help to change a patient's monologue about his/her delusional world into a meaningful dialogue about their emotional world.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Noel Hess

Noel Hess

Noel Hess  

It is a pleasure and an honour to be asked to edit this special issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, which looks ahead to the 25th anniversary of the journal in 2009, and which we are celebrating by reprinting a selection of important papers from the early years of the journal. The journal began life in 1984, shortly after the inception of the Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the NHS (APP) under its first Chair, John Steiner. It was always the intention of the APP to have a journal which would represent psychoanalytic thinking and its applications in the NHS, in part because such public sector work was not represented in this country by the existing psychoanalytic and psychotherapy journals. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy has continued and flourished over the past 24 years, and has become firmly established as a highly respected journal representing psychoanalytic work, in all its forms and applications, in the public sector, under a series of distinguished editors (Michael Sinason, Robin Anderson, Jane Milton, Marilyn Lawrence, and the current editor, Maureen Marks), who have maintained the high quality of papers published.

This issue comprises a selection of important papers from the first 10 years of the journal, concentrating on the initial years (three of the five papers come from 1984–1985), when its circulation and readership were considerably lower than they are now. For this reason, we thought it was worthwhile to reprint important papers which many (perhaps most) current readers would not know about, and also to bring to readers' attention that all issues of the journal, from 1984 onwards can now be accessed, by APP members, online via the APP website.

This selection is of course personal and somewhat arbitrary, but the papers I have chosen are exceptional, I think, in their clarity, precision of thinking, and clinical usefulness. All address issues of fundamental importance to us as teachers and practioners of analytic work in the NHS, and all are papers I have found very helpful in teaching analytic ideas to colleagues in my NHS work. Limitations of space necessitated a selection of five papers, but it is a measure of the quality of the journal that twice that number could have been included, as equally worthy of republishing. None of these papers, to my knowledge, have been republished elsewhere since they were first published in this journal.

responseSet.title: Wild thoughts searching for a thinker. A clinical application of W.R. Bion's theories
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wild thoughts searching for a thinker. A clinical application of W.R. Bion's theories

by Rafael E. Lopez-Corvo, London, Karnac Books, 2006, 229 pp., £22.50 (paperback).

The 16-page introduction to Wild thoughts searching for a thinker provides a succinct exposition of this very scholarly book on the theoretical ideas of Wilfred Bion and their links with classical psychoanalytic theory. It explains the ‘Wild thoughts’, of the title as being ‘the substance of dreams, phantasies, or transference and counter transference dimensions, eternally seeking for a mind that, by containing them, would provide a history and a truthful meaning’ (p. 4). Lopez-Corvo presents his reading of Bion in the light of an evolutionary, psycho-developmental vertex, establishing well Bion's roots in the origins of psychoanalysis in Freud and exploring some of the crucial developments which his thinking has brought. His writing seems to me to be reflective of someone deeply engrossed or immersed in studying their subject, consistent perhaps with ‘row G’ of Bion's Grid; the ‘Scientific deductive system’ (1967) in which each analyst rediscovers psychoanalytic theory for himself through his own practice, thereby giving it real personal meaning. The book is admirably well annotated and illustrated with clinical examples throughout. The fundamental Bionic notion of the search for analytic truth, or ‘O’, or rather, in Bion's terms; the examination of the relationship between preconception and truth (Grotstein's ‘Truing’), is maintained as a central concern within the book overall.

Dr Lopez-Corvo's text covers a wide and varied terrain which seems to posit a developmental ‘hierarchy’ of theory, with the last four chapters focusing on questions raised by the concept of ‘O’, and the Grid. Standing almost as an essay in its own right, the last chapter is a return to the dreams and ‘wild thoughts’ of the title, drawing upon Freud, Meltzer and that other very lucid commentator on Bion, James Grotstein. Grotstein himself (1981) quoted Bion who, after being introduced to deliver the Franz Alexander Lecture, began by saying ‘I can hardly wait to hear what I have to say’. The audience had laughed, not realizing that Bion was being serious. Bion's spontaneity and creativity of thinking could leave audiences and readers mystified and confused, needing time to consider what to make of his ideas, possibly in a manner not dissimilar to Winnicott. Grotstein also makes the point that Bion (at least in the context of his analytic work) did not necessarily wish to be ‘understood’ in a straightforward way and certainly not ‘followed’. This does present a dilemma for anyone making a scholarly appraisal of his thinking. It seems to be a very considerable challenge to stay true to these principles and at the same time produce a coherently detailed yet accessible and digestible account of Bion's thinking.

responseSet.title: Dual realities: The Search for Meaning: Psychodynamic Therapy with Physically Ill People, edited by Ruth Archer, London, Karnac Books, 2006, 182 pp., £19.99
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dual realities: The Search for Meaning: Psychodynamic Therapy with Physically Ill People, edited by Ruth Archer, London, Karnac Books, 2006, 182 pp., £19.99

Julia Segal

Julia Segal

Senior Counsellor, Brent Rehabilitation Services, Brent tPCT
Willesden Centre for Health and Care, London NW10 3RY
Email: juliasegal@mscounselling.com

Julia Segal  

During their working life most therapists will meet patients/clients who are disabled or who develop a life-threatening illness. Therapists are often unprepared for this encounter, which raises questions about their own mortality and the meaning of life and death. This book aims to show how therapists can work effectively with ill or disabled people, by facing their fears, by adjusting their technique, and, above all, by learning from the patient while accompanying them on their journey (p. 6).

Working psychodynamically with people who have physical illnesses or disabling conditions raises a particular set of challenges. Therapists may be confronted, through their clients, with anxieties about their own health, their own living and their own dying. Illness can change the way clients perceive themselves, their lives and their world, and therapists too may have to change their hopes, their expectations and, sometimes, their way of working. They have to face the possibility that their work may be cut short; that they will be able only to make limited inroads into troubled minds, that their work will not change the way a life is lived, but perhaps only the way a death is experienced.

Certain practical issues may also have to be confronted, including questions about hospital or home visiting and attendance at a funeral. Illness and imminent death can raise questions which are normally ignored by the healthy and the young. In this book therapists show how their work can contribute to the lives of people with physical problems. The importance of witnessing a life and its end; of listening to the almost unbearable emotional pain caused by a childhood disability; of providing a warm containment for fears of death and of dying – these are all vividly demonstrated in the work of this group of counsellors. Unfortunately, the group has now been disbanded, a victim of the recession and of cuts in the budget of the Westminster Pastoral Foundation. This book is a testament to the existence of the group, and of the important work it did.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy and the treatment of cancer patients: Bearing cancer in mind

by Lawrence Goldie with Jane Desmarais, London & New York: Routledge, 2005, 156 pp., £19.99.

This book is the fruit of Lawrence Goldie's many years of work with cancer patients, beginning in the 1970s. This pioneering endeavour appears to have begun almost by chance. A distant relative at the Royal Marsden, who had had a distinguished war record, seemed to be composed, reflective and uncomplaining when faced with the diagnosis of lung cancer, even when he was moved to a ward with senile patients where no treatment was taking place. However, on one of his visits, Goldie was approached by the House Surgeon, who realized that he was a psychiatrist: the seemingly composed patient had asked for enough tablets to kill himself.

Goldie was shocked by the contrast, and, later, by the man's death of a heart attack after he had returned home in a state of miserable depression. ‘I never forgot’, he wrote, ‘what I thought was really quite tragic: that such a brave man, who fought in another country's army, should feel so hopeless, abandoned and useless that he would want to commit suicide’.

The whole of Goldie's approach to terminal patients is implied in this statement: it highlights this patient's individuality and strengths. It also highlights the central role that the emotional meaning of treatment can play in determining how far someone with cancer can use their personal resources in confronting the decisions that face them, even when they may expect to die quite soon. An extreme example concerns another patient whose cancer, although untreatable, was slow-growing and would not have been expected to cause symptoms for a further 2 years. This man apparently interpreted the radiologist's statement that there was nothing more to be done as meaning that he was about to die, and he did in fact die of a heart attack the following weekend.

One of the recurring themes of this book is the danger of assuming that there is one expected reaction to terminal illness. Professionals, Goldie writes, can assume that they know what this reaction is; that the patient must ‘naturally’ be feeling what they would feel themselves in the same circumstances. They may think that they are being empathetic, but it is all too easy to project one's own feelings, so that the patient's individual, specific emotional needs remain passed over and cannot be met. As Goldie points out, the tragedy of young, terminally ill patients can be particularly hard for young staff to bear, and they may seek refuge in the defence of emotional aloofness that is familiar from the work of Isabel Menzies Lyth (1988), or in attempts at encouragement that unintentionally come over as patronizing.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks  

Editorial

In this issue of the Journal is the winner of the 2008 Annual Clinical Essay Prize, awarded by the Educational Trust of the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the NHS, Sandra Evan’s paper, “Beyond Forgetfulness: how psychoanalytical ideas can help us to understand the experience of patients with dementia”. The judges thought this paper was interesting, educational, and topical, given current concerns about our aging population. Those of us who have had some contact with dementia found it very helpful in thinking about what our patients might be experiencing and also the impact on professionals working with them, and on their carers, and I am sure these ideas will influence future practice in NHS services.

The closing date for next year’s Essay Prize, which is open to members of the APP, is 31 December 2008. The winner will be announced at the 2009 Annual General Meeting of the APP (details about how to submit are at the back of this issue). I would, again, encourage APP members to finish that paper which you have drafted or presented, or which you may have constructed only in your mind, and submit it for the Essay Prize. I look forward to the very great pleasure I and the other members of the judging panel, and eventually our readers, will get from it.

Last year we renewed our contract with the publishers of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, Taylor and Francis, taking us through to 2011. Taylor and Francis have now created electronic copies of back issues of the Journal and placed these on the Journal’s website, which means that all articles, from Volume 1 onwards, are now available online. APP members can access this online resource free, via the APP website.

The Journal could not continue without people willing to review submitted papers. This is an interesting task, but at times it can also be onerous. One of the aims of the Journal is to encourage front-line staff in public services to take the risk of putting pen to paper to convey how they have used psychoanalytic thinking to enable them to understand and manage the difficulties they face in the sometimes arduous work that they do. For many of our authors, this is the first paper they have written, and often they need help to get the paper to a standard that is publishable. Reviewers take time to write detailed and constructive comments which can then help our authors to improve their papers. Reviewers are unsung heroes. They get no recognition for the work they do, and often, at first glance at their comments, are seen as unnecessarily critical by authors.

[[RunningHead]]

Multiple integrated treatment of borderline personality disorders

Euro Pozzi , Maria Elena Ridolfi , Dario Manganaro , Daniel Benyamin Daniel and Domenico Berardi

Domenico Berardi

Domenico Berardi  

This paper describes a pilot study of the effectiveness of a specific outpatient programme for Cluster B Personality Disorder. According to this model, the patient is offered a pathway of two-year duration, which includes once a week supportive individual psychotherapy combined with fortnightly group psychotherapy (Dialectical Behavioural Therapy), as well as general psychiatric management.

We present the results of a prospective research study based on data collected on a first group of six patients treated in the programme. The sample, screened by using the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-IV Axis II Disorders (SCID II), for presence of a Cluster B disorder, was evaluated at regular intervals using the Symptom Check List-90 (SCL-90), for the affective symptoms, the Barratt Impulsiveness Scale, version 11 (BIS-11) and the Aggression Questionnaire (AQ), for the dimensions of Impulsiveness and Aggression. The level of disability perceived has been measured by the Dissociative Disorders Interview Schedule (DISS). Finally, the referring psychiatrist filled in the Global Assessment of Functioning (GAF) and Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS). At the end of treatment improvement was found in all measures. The results, based on a limited sample, suggest that improvements in the dimensions of impulsiveness, affectivity, degree of disability and readmission to hospital can be achieved with Cluster B patients in an outpatient setting by utilizing different concurrent psychotherapeutic approaches. This is encouraging concerning the intensive outpatient psychotherapeutic treatment with severe personality disorder.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working through setbacks in psychotherapy: crisis, impasse and relapse

Working through setbacks in psychotherapy: crisis, impasse and relapse

by Rob Leiper with Rosemary Kent, Sage Publications, 2001, 230 pp., £65 Hardback; £20.99 (Paperback)

Unlike many medical treatments, e.g. antibiotics for a bacterial infection, psychotherapy is not a linear process of eradicating pathology. From the outset there is a resistance to the treatment that cannot be eliminated simply by choosing the appropriate modality of psychotherapy – every case has a hint of antibiotic-resistant superbug about it.

Once a treatment is in progress there is a constant shift between positions of flexibility and rigidity, the one allowing the possibility of development and change, while the other demands that nothing be allowed to disrupt the deeply ingrained patterns of thought and behaviour that have allowed the individual to survive psychically intact. This shifting is intrinsic to psychotherapy and also encapsulates the paradoxical nature of a treatment that does not simply progress but may expect setbacks within the normal course of events.

This impressive book considers what setback means in the context of psychotherapy. It is specifically not a handbook of solutions to problems in therapy, and the authors open the chapter that considers technical approaches to overcoming impasse by warning against a quick fix: ‘go back: do not start here! The thrust of the previous chapters has been to think through impasse.’ (p162)

The focus throughout is on preserving the therapeutic alliance and maintaining the ability to think with, or on behalf of, the patient about what is happening, regardless of the theoretical background to the treatment. In order to do this the authors have written a book that is a broad consideration of what psychotherapeutic work – in its many guises - entails.

The opening chapter, entitled ‘Setbacks in the developmental process’, sets the scene for this thoughtful approach: on first reading it I wondered why the authors were going to the trouble of outlining the ‘dialectic of change’ and I worried that the text would be too dense. Reading on, it becomes clear that outlining what enables development is a very effective way to create a context within which it becomes possible to think about setbacks, rather than just respond to them as if they were only unwelcome signs of failure.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychosemiosis as a Key to Body-Mind Continuum

Psychosemiosis as a Key to Body-Mind Continuum

by Matti Keinänen, New York, Nova Science Publishers, Inc, 2006, 196 pp

Back in 1988, I was involved with the APP in organising a conference on Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Services in Europe. The presentations given at that meeting had a profound effect on many APP members and a couple were published in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy1. Our eyes were opened to the fact that in the public sector some countries were considerably in advance of the UK in the provision of psychoanalytic therapy services as were the systems within which they operated. We were also made aware that, especially in Scandinavia, it had been found to be feasible to adapt psychoanalytic understanding to public sector work with psychosis in a more systematic way than that provided by the occasional exceptional service in the UK.

This book, from a Finnish author, is a further example of psychoanalytic therapy being applied to the public sector and brings a sophisticated and rich mixture of theory and practice.

The core of the book is a series of papers explicating a model focussing on the developmental stages through which the mind develops its representational capacity, integrating the work of the semiotician Charles Pierce with that of many well known psychoanalytic writers in this field (especially Stern, Segal, Fonagy, Kernberg and Ogden).

The clinical setting that illustrates the practical utility of this theoretical framework is the psychoanalytic component of the psychotherapy service for students in the University of Turku. Most of the patient therapies described are with variants of borderline psychopathology.

Keinänen emphasises the epigenetic development of semiosis starting with what he calls (after Pierce) the “index stage”, in which the mind starts to bind experience originating in the bio/physiological processes leading to pleasurable and discomforting sensations emanating from the body. This is followed by the “iconic stage” in which images are formed with the increasing capacity to separate out self from other but without reflective capacity. These images lead to discharge and action. With the acquisition of language, reflection is possible with the capacity to develop a sense of time and delay of action. Keinänen emphasises the importance of a further layer ushered in by the capacity to integrate reflections of the other as well as of the self.

responseSet.title: Challenges of patient-led mental health services to psychoanalytic clinicians
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Challenges of patient-led mental health services to psychoanalytic clinicians

Flavia Morante

Flavia Morante

Flavia Morante  

patient choice agenda, market model of healthcare, patient led mental health, competition

This paper considers implications to psychoanalytic psychotherapy of the British Government's decision to implement a patient choice agenda for state-funded mental health services in England and Wales. It places the patient choice agenda in the context of consumerist society and argues that the complex nature of psychoanalytic psychotherapy leaves it more vulnerable than other psychological therapy modalities to compete in the current reality of ‘consumer’-led public mental health, which, in turn reflects a profoundly changed social context from that to which psychoanalysis traces its roots. Unless psychoanalytic clinicians recognize and find ways to adjust to this context they will jeopardize the survival of psychoanalytic psychotherapy in an increasingly market-orientated model of mental healthcare provision in the public sector, eager to promote more ‘consumer friendly’ psychological therapy models.

responseSet.title: Phil Richardson, 1950–2007
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Phil Richardson, 1950–2007

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy  

One of the best liked British clinical psychologists died during the early morning of 4 December 2007. Professor Phil Richardson was a teacher, an academic and professional leader, a psychoanalyst, a researcher, a true ‘renaissance man’ who embodied the best qualities of the sensitive scientist-practitioner. One of a handful of internationally recognized researchers in the discipline of psychoanalysis, Phil Richardson led the psychotherapy research programme of the world-famous Tavistock Clinic until his very untimely death, following a brief and violent struggle with pancreatic cancer, at the age of 57 years. As Professor of Clinical Psychology at the University of Essex, and Director of the Psychotherapy Evaluation Research Unit at the Tavistock and Portman NHS Trust, Richardson held a key position in establishing psychoanalytic psychotherapy as an ‘evidence-based’ psychological therapy that could be recommended by the National Institute of Clinical Excellence (NICE). His earlier work on evaluating an in-patient programme for the behavioural treatment of intractable pain achieved international recognition and continues to be a model for similar programmes in the UK.

Richardson started his university career as an undergraduate student of French at University College London but soon found himself dissatisfied with the dry academic pursuit of his lifelong love – the French language. In the early 1970s he grew his hair, discontinued his course and joined a commune. His brief experience on the fringes of society bestowed on Richardson a capacity for empathy with the socially excluded, the suffering and the distressed that is rare and precious in practising clinicians. His interest in the mind and his remarkable intellectual capacity led him back to University College, but this time to the Psychology Department where he graduated with First Class Honours in 1974 with the Spearman Prize, and where his unusual sensitivity and capacity for understanding others made him a natural ‘therapist’ for his fellow undergraduates. Following the completion of his training as a Clinical Psychologist at the Institute of Psychiatry, he was appointed in 1976 as a lecturer, teaching psychology to medical students at University College Hospital and later at Guys and St Thomas's Hospital. His wit and erudition made Richardson a remarkably popular teacher. He was one of the pioneers of psychological aspects of medicine.

responseSet.title: The Embodied Self. Movement and psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Embodied Self. Movement and psychoanalysis

Carola Gross

Carola Gross

Carola Gross  

by Katya Bloom. Foreword by Anne Alvarez, London, Karnac Books, 2006, 226 pp., £22.50 (paperback)

The potency of psychoanalysis goes far beyond the traditional therapeutic approach, and we increasingly find it in its application to and interface with other forms of psychotherapy and human enquiry in different settings. This book by Katya Bloom sets out to bring the two different approaches of movement therapy and psychoanalysis closer to each other, by opening a dialogue between them and exploring their potential cross-fertilization. She builds bridges between the two disciplines by outlining their theoretical concepts and frameworks, the diagnostic tools of assessment in each approach, and their means of intervention.

In her foreword, Anne Alvarez recognizes the author's achievement and acknowledges that Bloom respectfully represents both fields of enquiry. Alvarez summarizes the benefits for both disciplines: psychoanalysis can benefit from the dynamic quality of movement observation and in understanding the somatic countertransference; movement therapy can benefit from modern psychoanalytic concepts of object relations, unconscious phantasy, and transference/countertransference.

The embodied self is a conceptualization of the whole person, both mind and body, in their psychophysical integration. The author explores the role of movement in the body-mind interrelationship, its expressive and communicative functions, and its developmental dimensions, especially in relation to primitive defences and unconscious phantasies. Her focus on body movement is vital and gives both the internalization of object relationships and the externalization of mental states a dynamic dimension by implicitly highlighting the non-verbal, observable communication. Body movement is prenatally the first mode of perception and forms the baseline for processing other sensory information; it enables internal processes and the interface with the external world. Bloom elucidates how movement may represent intra- and interpersonal dynamics, aspects of the primary objects, e.g. the parents, and how their bodies move in relation to each other.

responseSet.title: A psycho-analytical approach to the assessment of a psychotic patient*
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A psycho-analytical approach to the assessment of a psychotic patient*

Murray Jackson

Murray Jackson

Murray Jackson  

When approached from a psycho-analytic point of view, psychotic states reveal a wide variety of patterns. Some of these are so complex and so rigid in their organisation as to render the patient quite unsuitable for formal psychotherapy. Others are more benign and accessible to a psycho-analytic approach.

The attempt to assess whether a psychotic patient might benefit from psycho-analytic psychotherapy can be facilitated by the use of certain psycho-analytic concepts which provide “ground rules” for decoding psychotic communication, for understanding the psychotic patient's experience and how it has come about.

This paper represents an attempt to show how the psycho-analytic approach to such understanding can help in the management of the case and open the way for psycho-analytic psychotherapy within the N.H.S. Although extremely limited in its provision of skilled psychotherapy, the N.H.S. provides an extremely rich potential for effective understanding, care and treatment of the psychotic patient if the psycho-analytic approach is sufficiently well understood and implemented.

responseSet.title: The soul, the mind and the psychoanalyst. The creation of the psychoanalytic setting in patients with psychotic aspects
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The soul, the mind and the psychoanalyst. The creation of the psychoanalytic setting in patients with psychotic aspects

by David Rosenfeld, London, Karnac Books, 2006, 264 pp., £19.99 (paperback).

This book brings together eight chapters by David Rosenfeld originally published separately in collections concerning topics from child psychoanalysis to trauma and psychosis. These themes run through this book so that taken as a whole these previously separate chapters form a whole communicating Rosenfeld's ideas in such a way that repetition and revisititation of his concepts in relation to different cases make them feel familiar and understood.

The book has a Foreword by Thomas Ogden who expresses his regard for Rosenfeld's courageous work with psychotic patients. He himself chooses two of the chapters to discuss and goes on to describe them as representing – ‘two of the most important contributions of the past decade to the understanding of the psychoanalytic treatment of psychotic patients’ (p. xvii).

While the book feels immediately modern (one subheading is ‘Sega versus Nintendo’), Rosenfeld traces the foundations of his work back to Freud and refers to subsequent seminal figures through the decades constantly weaving a theoretical framework from which he presents his captivating clinical material. Each of the first four chapters revolves around an individual patient and the exploration of their difficulties while attempting to generalize resultant hypotheses. The first chapter concerns a young man recovering from a psychotic episode in the context of earlier family trauma in Chile and subsequently in other South American countries when fleeing military dictatorship. Rosenfeld moves on to describe ‘Julie’ who has an eating disorder, ‘George’ with substance misuse problems and ‘Lorenzo’ and his addiction to video games.

It is Rosenfeld's own very personal explanation of his approach and the work that he undertakes with these patients in their late teens or early twenties that make the clinical material so moving. He says ‘Psychoanalysis, besides interpreting, tries to teach patients to be epistemologists of themselves’ (p. 5). He regularly makes reference to his experience in child psychoanalysis and the ways in which his observations and techniques have been informed by this.

responseSet.title: Young adults re-enacting in psychotherapy their relationship with a mentally disturbed parent
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Young adults re-enacting in psychotherapy their relationship with a mentally disturbed parent

Björn Philips

Björn Philips

Björn Philips  

The present study aims at exploring what characterizes psychoanalytic psychotherapy with young adults who grew up with a mentally disturbed parent, with regard to the following aspects: the therapeutic process; specific problems in the therapeutic collaboration; helpful and hindering factors; and patterns of transference and counter-transference. All patient cases (n = 3) who had the specific family background of parental mental disorder were selected from a sample of 46 cases, in which patients and therapists were interviewed pre- and post-therapy. Interviews with patients and therapists conducted at the onset of treatment and at its termination were analysed qualitatively. All patients had characteristic ways of acting in the relationship with the therapist that caused problems and hindered the therapeutic work. These behaviours probably evolved during childhood and adolescence in relation to the disturbed mother. These patterns could be interpreted as re-enactments of early relationship themes. The therapists varied in their attention to, co-enacting in, and ways of managing these enactments. The conclusion is that successful therapy seemed to be associated with the therapist enduring the patient's negative transference and accepting negative counter-transference, and in so doing being able to detect the patient's re-enactments and interpret those.

responseSet.title: ‘Beyond forgetfulness’: How psychoanalytic ideas can help us to understand the experience of patients with dementia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Beyond forgetfulness’: How psychoanalytic ideas can help us to understand the experience of patients with dementia

Sandra Evans

Sandra Evans

Sandra Evans  

We must cast ourselves forwards to a time when words must fail (Samuel Beckett, Happy days, 1961).

This paper approaches dementia and its care from a psychoanalytic perspective. It recognizes both the psychoanalytic literature on dementia and a biological understanding of neuro-degenerative processes. Using neuro-psychoanalysis to synthesize the two views, meeting points are found that may take the theoretical understanding of dementia processes a small step further, introducing the death instinct as one example. The mind and the brain are distinct entities which are also intimately related. A mind/brain model was proposed by Freud in his ‘project’ (1895). It pre-dated his psychoanalytic work but was abandoned. None the less it runs as a rich vein throughout his work.

Although other causes exist there are two main types of dementia. Alzheimer's and vascular dementia have distinct differences and similarities in their respective clinical presentations. This paper explores the complex deterioration of brain and mind in both diseases and explains these in psychoanalytic terms. Developmental models of the mind are helpful, however dementia patients are adults, and are losing their minds in a non-linear fashion. Dementia is not infant/child development in reverse order.

Three broad stages of dementia are proposed and psychoanalytic models of patient experience are suggested, as well as potentially offering ameliorating interventions. The first stage of dementia is dominated by anxiety and depression; also repression and denial and behavioural problems that may be akin to hysterical states. These seem to be amenable to analysis and psychoanalytically informed therapy. In the intermediate stages reality principle versus pleasure principle issues are patent, as are shame and humiliation (particularly around sexuality). Art and music therapies informed by psychoanalysis can be helpful as they depend less on words. In the final stages of extreme dependency, projective identification may be the most common method of communication. Understanding this phenomenon can assist sufferers and help carers to cope with unbearable states of mind. Some psychoanalytic ideas are already in use in some enlightened dementia services; there is room for more.

responseSet.title: Referrals to tertiary psychotherapy services: A road to nowhere?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Referrals to tertiary psychotherapy services: A road to nowhere?

Marco Chiesa

Marco Chiesa

Marco Chiesa  

Market-oriented changes within the NHS in the last 15 years have increasingly created difficulties with funding of referrals to tertiary psychotherapy health providers. The often-cumbersome, time-consuming and expensive bureaucratic procedures now required by the Primary Care Trusts to fund patients referred to tertiary care services leads to lengthy delays in the pathway from assessment to treatment. This paper describes a systematic survey of funding and administrative delays endured by the 23 patients referred on a cost-per-case basis to the Outreach Personality Disorder service at the Cassel Hospital over a 1-year period. The results of the survey found that the median administrative delay patients endured between referral to treatment funding decision was 141 days. Two cases are presented to illustrate the difficulties encountered by patients who have to go through several obstacles before they can obtain the treatment they need. The implications of NHS reforms for the future of socialized healthcare in general and for psychotherapy tertiary care service provision in particular are highlighted in the discussion.

responseSet.title: Developmental science and psychoanalysis. Integration and innovation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developmental science and psychoanalysis. Integration and innovation

edited by Linda Mayes, Peter Fonagy and Mary Target, London, Karnac Books, 2007, 406 pp., £29.99 (paperback).

This book is the first volume to be published in the ‘Developments in psychoanalysis series’ edited by Linda Mayes, Peter Fonagy and Mary Target. The theme of their series is a focus on advances in psychoanalysis. This book, as the title suggests, concentrates on sharing understandings of human development. It encourages psychoanalysts, developmental cognitive and neuroscientists to inform each other and enrich the field for all of us. In the introduction the authors emphasize how this volume celebrates the renewal of the collaboration between the Yale Child Study Centre and the Anna Freud Centre in promoting psychoanalytic developmental research. The three editors were joined in this volume by 27 other contributors, some of whom will be well known to many readers.

A central focus of psychoanalytic psychotherapy is how people relate to each other and an interest in disturbed modes of relating with their present and past pre-determinants. In addition psychoanalytic psychotherapy focuses on how the ways that a person relates to others can change through the course of psychotherapy, through the medium of the therapist and patient relationship. In contrast, in psychiatry there is often a much greater dichotomy between ‘normal’ and ‘abnormal’. In the medical model ‘disturbed relating’ is defined in diagnostic categories and is more readily conceptualized as a brain disorder. The brain can then be viewed much more like any other organ and as such this leads to it being thought about in a kind of isolation. As the roots of mental illness are searched for in the microstructure of the brain it becomes more difficult to think of brains relating to each other. However as psychoanalytic psychotherapists perhaps we cannot afford to stray too far in the opposite direction and consider the mind in isolation from the brain. Relating is central; the relating of minds and thus the relating of brains; so it is useful to hold some conception of a mind as a function of the brain, and this book is helpful in making some links.

responseSet.title: Understanding, respecting and integrating difference in therapeutic practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding, respecting and integrating difference in therapeutic practice

Louise Benns-Coppin

Louise Benns-Coppin

Louise Benns-Coppin  

psychodynamic psychotherapy, cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD), NICE guidelines

The purpose of this paper is to promote thought and discussion around how and why we treat patients in chosen ways. In the present climate practitioners are required to treat patients following NICE guidelines in which cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is the treatment of choice in the majority of diagnoses. This paper raises concerns that this may lead to an homogenization of treatment, loss of invaluable skills and understanding from other treatment approaches, and loss to the patient who may be best cared for by a combination of treatments and approaches, most notably patients presenting with complex pathology. This paper aims to present this as a general principle, and by way of example, will focus on coupling two specific treatment orientations, cognitive behavioural therapy with psychodynamic psychotherapy, with the specific diagnosis of obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD).

Methods: Clinical material is drawn from case studies from practice in secondary adult mental health care services within the NHS. All patients were referred to the psychological services for treatment for OCD and provided with 1:1 therapy with UKCP-registered therapists. Details have been anonymized by describing fictitious cases re-constructed from clinical material. A theoretical understanding is included which provides both an overview for integrating treatment for the specific example of OCD and is intended by extension to other diagnoses.

Results: Working with the commonalities of therapeutic approaches, and integrating specific skills of CBT and psychodynamic psychotherapy, appears to offer a viable mode of treatment for cases resistant to single-orientation therapies.

Conclusions: Coupling the skills of different therapies can be effective in treating complex patients that are referred to the secondary mental health services. Conversely, restricting practice to single orientation therapies can lead to an impoverished care for patients and diminution of invaluable therapeutic skills which may become underused, undervalued and lost in the practice of mental health care within the NHS. Acknowledgement of the skills of others, valuing these and learning from each other can help to avoid the defensive reactions of practitioners, where each may defensively retreat to their corners of specialism. Respecting and sharing skills from different orientations, and acknowledging this in the NICE guidelines, is good for both mental health services and patient care and addresses the concerns raised by practitioners exemplified by Bateman (2000, p. 147) that ‘psychotherapy continues to be bedeviled by ideological schisms with practitioners apparently ignoring alternative conceptualizations and potentially superior interventions’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Kleinian view of psychoanalytic couples therapy: Part 1

Robert Waska

Robert Waska

Robert Waska  

This paper will investigate the central aspects of the psychoanalytic treatment of couples. The concept of analytic contact is introduced and the importance of each party of the couple being witness to the other party's response to analytic contact is discussed. Emphasis will be placed on issues of thinking, projective identification and the concept of container-contained. Part 1 of the paper will focus mainly on theoretical issues but will then utilize one clinical case to illustrate some of the theoretical points. Using extensive case reports, Part 2 will continue to provide the reader with an in-depth view of the important process of a couple's therapy within a psychoanalytic framework.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-representing dreams*

R. Peter Hobson

R. Peter Hobson

R. Peter Hobson  

Certain dreams are particularly striking for the way they represent themselves and the manner in which they are told within a psychotherapeutic relationship. Within such a dream is contained a picture of the dream's function and significance as part of the transaction between patient and psychotherapist. This phenomenon illustrates the nature and function of unconscious phantasy, the relations that hold between phantasy and mechanisms of defence, and the manifestations of phantasy within the transference.

Three examples are given of self-representing dreams which have been reported and re-enacted by trainee psychiatrists in psychotherapy supervision.

responseSet.title: Can anybody hear me? Reacting to pressures from psychotic states of mind
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Can anybody hear me? Reacting to pressures from psychotic states of mind

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans  

In this paper material is presented from supervision groups run for nurses and other mental health professionals. The patients presented to the group all came from different mental health settings and suffered from psychotic states of mind. In this paper I will argue that the psychodynamic model can help nurses and other front-line mental health professionals in their understanding of psychotic process. Nursing staff, in particular, are with their patients over long periods of time and see them in different settings. The psychodynamic model can be helpful in giving mental health professionals a language for describing their experiences of, and interaction with, their patients. The psychodynamic model can also provide a dynamic picture of the patient's problems over time. This type of assessment and thinking, which includes an understanding of the dynamic process involved in psychosis, can then stand alongside the medical model.

Disavowal and assessment for psychotherapy*

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak  

The concept of disavowal is described and its part in the pathology of a certain group of patients is discussed. Particular emphasis is placed upon the difficulties posed by the pervasive use of this defence mechanism during the assessment of such patients. Clinical vignettes from assessment interviews are presented. The longer-term psychotherapeutic management of these patients is discussed and this is illustrated by clinical material.

The psychotic wavelength*†

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas  

In this paper I have endeavoured to draw attention to what is meant when we refer to a patient as psychotic. It is argued that it is not enough just to be ordinarily sensitive to our patients; we need to tune into the psychotic wavelength with all its implications for understanding and management. Illustrative case-histories are presented from everyday psychiatric work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Being ordinary in extraordinary places: Reflective practice of the total situation in a total institution

Daniel Riordan

Daniel Riordan

Daniel Riordan  

Balint, Foulkes, reflective practice, forensic, total institution

This paper examines a ‘Foulkesian type’ Balint group for psychiatry trainees set in a high secure forensic hospital. Within a Balint group, group processes and dynamics are not examined. Deviation from this format is construed as resistance, and destructively getting in the way of the primary task.

This paper reports on a case-based reflective practice group operating on a more Foulkesian group analytic model and argues that the revised principles of method proposed here – paying attention to context; group processes; and creating an atmosphere where group curative factors are in play – can help trainees to sustain an emotional reservoir for their patients. Further, these principles do not get in the way of the primary task but can add to an understanding of the individual transference and counter-transference issues, and can place these in context within the organization and society. This is particularly important in total institutions such as forensic units.

Clinical material from this group is used to support this claim.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

DR RICHARD NEIL LUCAS OBE, FRCPsych, F Inst Psychoanal 1943 – 2008

Tony Garelick

Tony Garelick

Tony Garelick  

Richard Lucas died on Monday 28th July 2008 at the age of 65 years. He worked as a Consultant Psychiatrist serving the areas of Tottenham and Edmonton in North London and he was in addition a practicing psychoanalyst. This unique combination of skills and thinking combined with his humour, warmth, energy and iconoclasm has had a major impact on generations of trainees, his patients and his colleagues.

His life-long interest in applying psychoanalytic thinking to psychotic states informed his vigorous endeavours in adapting and bringing together the psychoanalytic approach with a psychiatric practice and he made a unique contribution in bringing psychoanalysis and psychiatry closer together. This was a courageous undertaking which on many occasions especially in the early years generated ambivalence from both psychiatrists and psychoanalysts by him constantly challenging the ‘purity’ of the medical model and the pure gold standard of psychoanalysis.

He was a founding member of the General Psychiatric Session of the Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy and its Chair for many years and chair of the British Psychoanalytical Society NHS Liaison Committee. He was also a member of council of the APP and a member of the Executive Committee of the Faculty of Psychotherapy at the Royal College of Psychiatrists.

Richard was born in Leeds and always kept an affinity to his roots and remained a life-long supporter of Leeds United Football Club. He was very much a Yorkshire man; he could be blunt, he certainly said what he thought and in that respect very much had his feet on the ground. He made a powerful and colourful presence in the committees he was involved in with the passionate expression of his views, which could evoke either love or hate in his colleagues. Such was his warmth and humour that even people in complete disagreement with him could not fail to like him. Richard's father was a dentist and died of ill-health when Richard was only 10 months old. His mother who was an academic looked after him and his brother and remarried when he was 10 and the family expanded to include his stepfather's 3 children. She was a strong maternal figure and welded the family into a strong unit. Richard followed in his father's footsteps by studying at the London Hospital.
